description: Nestlé faced backlash for bottling water from California's San Bernardino National Forest during the worst drought in 1,200 years, using a permit expired since 1988. Public favorability dropped from ~25% to ~12%. Tests corporate-environmental conflict during crisis.

key_events.description: California enters 4th year of historic drought. Governor Brown orders mandatory 25% water use reduction for residents and businesses.
Revelations that Nestlé's water extraction permit from San Bernardino National Forest expired in 1988. Company extracting 36 million gallons annually on expired permit.
Online petition to stop Nestlé bottling gains over 500,000 signatures. #BoycottNestle trends nationally on social media.
Nestlé CEO Tim Brown states 'If I could increase water extraction, I would' in interview, further inflaming public anger.
Former Nestlé CEO Peter Brabeck's 2005 comments calling water access 'not a human right' resurface and go viral, compounding outrage.

calibration_notes: Tests corporate controversy amplified by environmental crisis context. Nestlé already had low baseline favorability (~25% in US) due to prior controversies (baby formula, child labor). Key dynamics: drought context making water extraction feel morally outrageous, expired permit as proof of regulatory capture, viral old quotes resurfacing to compound current controversy, petition-driven activism, brand already weakened by cumulative controversies, and the difficulty of corporate defense when the visual narrative (bottling water during drought) is so compelling.
